summary: Jeff Atwood reviews 'Coders at Work' by Peter Seibel, praising it as a rare example of genuinely skilled interviewing applied to influential programmers. He contrasts it with the typical low-quality internet interviews that serve as content filler. Atwood suggests the book could serve as a hiring filter—candidates who can't identify any of the 15 programmers interviewed should be an immediate no-hire. He also pays tribute to the book's inspiration, Susan Lammers' 1986 'Programmers at Work,' noting how both books demonstrate the timelessness of programming wisdom. The post concludes that understanding software requires studying not just code, but the people who create it.
key_points: Most internet interviews are lazy content filler, but 'Coders at Work' represents genuinely skilled journalism applied to programming luminaries
The book could serve as a job interview filter—inability to recognize any of the 15 featured programmers suggests a lack of engagement with the field
Great programming books can remain relevant for decades, as demonstrated by 'Programmers at Work' from 1986
The focus on people rather than just languages makes 'Coders at Work' more compelling than similar interview collections
Software development is fundamentally about people, not just code
money_quotes: If the internet has perfected anything, it's the art of the crappy, phoned-in, half-assed email 'interview.'
The purpose of most of these interviews is quick and dirty content filler with semi-famous folk spouting off whatever random thoughts they happen to have in their head at that exact moment.
Interviews take an enormous amount of time and an enormous amount of legitimate, skilled journalistic effort to get right. Almost nobody does.
If, after reading what these brilliant programmers have to say, you aren't motivated to research some programming topic mentioned inside, pack it in, because you aren't even trying any more.
It is a testament to the timelessness of not just code, but the art of coding.
Computers are mostly a reflection of the people using them.
In the art of software development, studying code isn't enough: you have to study the people behind the software, too.
key_insight: Understanding software development requires studying the programmers themselves, not just their code—and recognizing the influential figures in your field is a basic marker of professional engagement.
